text: The video shows gross sacculation of the small bowel due to chronic stricturing associated with Crohn's disease.

The terminal ileum and right colon are the commonest region of disease activity in Crohn's. Clinical presentation may be due to acute inflammation or more chronic formation of fibrostenotic strictures or of fistulae. Anatomical location of strictures reflects the distribution of disease activity.

A fifty-eight year old man presented to his family doctor with a thirty year history of abdominal cramps and intermittent bloating, predominantly postprandially. Upper GI endoscopy was unremarkable but ulcers were noted in the terminal ileum and a granuloma seen in a rectal biopsy. Small bowel stricturing was identified on CT. A diagnosis of stricture in Crohn's disease was made but the patient refused surgery.

Subsequently, he sought attention as his symptoms had deteriorated over a year. He had no other medical history of note and was then referred to our institution. An MR enterogram showed multiple distal ileal strictures with proximal gross sacculation. No enhancement was seen in the strictures, suggesting chronicity.
During the laparoscopy, the surgeon performs a thorough examination of the small bowel, starting from the ileocecal junction and proceeding to the duodenojejunal (DJ) junction.
The video shows several dense stricturing masses in the mid to distal ileum, accompanied by gross chronic sacculation of the intervening segments.
The surgeon delivers the diseased segment of the bowel through a short incision by extending the umbilical port site, while the proximal jejunum appears normal.

The surgeon performs a single resection of the atonic sacculated segments between strictures, which have a diameter of eleven centimeters. A side-to-side stapled anastomosis is then completed to join the resected bowel ends.

During a relaparoscopy, the surgeon performs a second small bowel walk to inspect the bowel after constructing the side-to-side stapled anastomosis. The surgical field shows the completed anastomosis and surrounding bowel segments.

At outpatient follow-up six months later, he reported a substantial improvement in quality of life and had gained five kilograms in weight. Pathological examination of the specimen showed patchy chronic inflammation with mucosal ulceration, fissuring ulceration and transmural lymphoid aggregates. Non-caseating granulomata were seen at submucosal and intramuscular locations.
